elements.sent-2411-001: In a system of two-dimensional electrons, a combination of broken symmetry, interactions, and nontrivial topology can conspire to give rise to a nonlinear transport regime, where electric current density scales as the square of electric field.
elements.sent-2411-002: This regime has become a venue for exciting discoveries such as the nonlinear Hall effect and diode-like nonreciprocal transport.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, interpretation of experimental data is challenging in the nonlinear regime as DC transport is described by a rank-3 conductivity tensor with 6 free parameters.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here, we resolve this challenge by analytically solving for the nonlinear potential distribution across the disk sample for an arbitrary linear and nonlinear conductivity tensors.
elements.sent-2411-005: This allows us to unambiguously extract all components of the nonlinear tensor from experimental measurement.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using this novel tool, we identify giant nonlinear Hall effect in Bernal bilayer graphene.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our methodology provides the first systematic framework for interpreting nonlinear transport and uncovers a new route towards understanding quasi-2D materials.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{Fig0v16.pdf} \caption{ {\bf{Nonlinear voltage response due to different components of the nonlinear conductivity tensor.}} (a) An optical image of the sample used in this work. The BBG is shown in purple (sample and contacts), blue is the dielectric substrate underneath the sample. The scale bar denotes 2 $\mu$m. (b) Schematic cross section of the BBG heterostructure. (c) Schematic of the angle-resolved measurement setup for $V_{\perp}$ (top) and $V_{\parallel}$ (bottom) in a disk-shaped sample. (d-e) The expected nonlinear voltage response under different measurement configurations for different nonlinear tensors: (d) $V_{\perp}$ of a tensor containing only $\tilde{\sigma}_{xxx}$ component, and (e) $V_{\parallel}$ of a tensor containing only the nonlinear Hall component. For illustration we took $\mathcal{C}$ along the $x-$axis. Three columns show a breakdown of Eqn.~\ref{main_eq:marvelous}: the left column shows vector map $\vec{V}_1=\tilde{\mathcal{E}}^{(2)}(\br')$, the middle column shows $\vec{V}_2=\nabla_{\br'}\left(G_{N}(\br_\bA,\br')-G_{N}(\br_\bB,\br')\right)$, the right column shows their scalar product, $\vec{V}_1\cdot\vec{V}_2$. Top and bottom rows correspond to current source located at $\phi=\pi/2$ and $\phi=\pi$, respectively. (f) The angle dependence of $V_{\perp}$ (top) and $V_{\parallel}$ (bottom) for the scenario described by panel (d) with $\tilde{\sigma}_{xxx}$ as the only nonzero tensor component. (g) and (f) The angle dependence of $V_{\parallel}$ (top) and $V_{\perp}$ (bottom) for the scenario described by panel (e) with the nonlinear Hall conductivity as the only nonzero tensor component. } \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-008: The Hall effect is one of the most studied phenomena in condensed matter physics.
elements.sent-2411-009: In its linear form, it relates the electrical potential difference perpendicular to the electrical current via a direct proportionality.
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite being more than a century-old there's still room for new discoveries as exemplified by the recent observation of the fractional quantum anomalous Hall effect in 2D van der Waals moire heterostructures \cite{Park2023_FCI,Lu2024_FCI,Fan_Xu_PRX_FCI,Zeng2023_FCI .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Beyond the linear response regime, a recent theoretical proposal pointed to a novel form of Hall effect, where the electric current in the nonlinear regime is always perpendicular to the local electric field ~\cite{Sodemann_Fu and microscopically induced by a Berry curvature dipole (BCD).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Exploring the transport response in the nonlinear regime, including the nonlinear Hall effect, opens new pathways to investigate exotic electronic orders emerging from the interplay between correlation and topology, which has attracted intense research efforts \cite{Sodemann_Fu,KinFai2019,Pablo2019,GaoScience2023,Han2024,JamesLeo2024NatMat,KoenigDzeroLevchenkoPesin,Du2021NatComm,Du2021,KaplanPRL2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: While the concept of nonlinear transport holds promise of new scientific discoveries as well as novel technological applications \cite{IsobeSciAdv2020,ZhangTerahertzPNAS2021 , so far the field has many open questions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We focus on two spatial dimensions where, in general, $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$ has 4 independent components while the DC limit nonlinear conductivity tensor $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ has 6.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The linear Hall conductivity $\sigma_H$ corresponds to the antisymmetric component of $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$, while the remaining three specify the standard dissipative (Ohmic) response, its anisotropy and the direction of principal axes.
elements.sent-2411-017: The nonlinear Hall current is most conveniently expressed in a coordinate-free form as $\bj^{\text{nl _{\text{Hall =\hat{\bz}\timesE \left( \mathcal{C \cdot E \right)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The nonlinear Hall conductivity can therefore be completely specified by a two-component vector $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Unlike its linear counterpart, non-zero $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ requires inversion symmetry to be broken.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This is evident from Eq. \eqref{Ohms_law given that both $j$ and E are odd under inversion.}
elements.sent-2411-021: As we explain later in the text, it also requires out-of plane rotational symmetry to be completely broken i.e. it would be prohibited in the presence on an $n$-fold symmetry for any $n>1$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike its linear counterpart \cite{Onsager1931 , the nonlinear Hall effect does not require the time-reversal (TR) symmetry to be broken \cite{Sodemann_Fu,MorimotoNagaosa2018SciReps,Du2021,MichishitaNagaosaPRB2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Since nonlinear response reflects symmetry breaking to which its linear counterpart may be insensitive, the observation of nonlinear transport response highlights new opportunities to investigate the rich interplay between correlations, broken symmetries, and topology underlying strongly correlated 2D electrons.
elements.sent-2411-024: For example, proposed microscopic mechanisms of nonlinear electrical response range from scattering induced by disorder (extrinsic effects) \cite{KoenigDzeroLevchenkoPesin,Du2021,Du2021NatComm,AtenciaNHE2023 or interactions \cite{MorimotoNagaosa2018SciReps to topology \cite{Sodemann_Fu or quantum geometry \cite{Gao2014PRLNHEmetric,Wang2021PRL,MichishitaNagaosaPRB2022,AtenciaNHE2023,AgarwalQG2023PRB,KaplanPRL2024 (intrinsic effects).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, it remains an outstanding challenge to properly analyze nonlinear transport data and to extract the components of $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ from an experiment without ambiguity.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Presently, experiments designed to obtain the nonlinear transport response in quasi-2D heterostructures \cite{KinFai2019,Pablo2019,GaoScience2023,JamesLeo2024NatMat involve measurements of nonlinear potential difference between two contacts, which are either aligned with the direction of injected current or perpendicular to it.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In previous reports, transverse nonlinear voltage difference was often considered as evidence of nonlinear Hall effect ~\cite{Pablo2019,KinFai2019,GaoScience2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, equating the nonlinear Hall effect with the transverse transport response may lead to erroneous conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-029: As is well known, a transverse response in the linear regime can result from a reduced rotational symmetry even for $\sigma_H=0$ ~\cite{Wu2017nematic .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In the nonlinear regime, the richer structure of $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ unlocks an even more diverse range of possibilities to generate a voltage difference transverse to the input current direction, some of which can occur even at $\mathcal{C =0$ and are therefore manifestly unrelated to the nonlinear Hall effect (this is illustrated in the Fig. \ref{fig1 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: A genuine identification of the nonlinear Hall effect therefore requires detailed and quantitative understanding of signatures of each independent component of the nonlinear conductivity tensor $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this work we utilize Bernal bilayer graphene (BBG) samples shaped in disk geometry and for the first time unambiguously extract the structure of nonlinear conductivity tensor.
elements.sent-2411-033: This is achieved in two steps.
elements.sent-2411-034: First, by analytically determining the distribution of nonlinear electrical potential across the disk-shaped sample with an arbitrary (uniform) $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$, $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$, and for a current $I$ injected at an arbitrary source (S) and removed at an arbitrary drain (D) at the perimeter of the disk.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Second, by employing angle-resolved transport measurements in both $I$ and $I^2$ regimes -- with a large number of source-drain-contact configurations shown in Figs.
elements.sent-2411-036: The new capability allows us identify the giant nonlinear Hall effect with record values of nonlinear conductivity exceeding previously reported values for giant nonlinearities in graphene-based structures \cite{He2022 and exceeding the values expected from known microscopic mechanisms by 3 to 7 orders of magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-038: We first demonstrate the possibility to generate transverse nonlinear response in the absence of nonlinear Hall conductivity i.e. for $\mathcal{C =0$, illustrating the challenge we aim to address.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Second, we present our solution which makes use of three basis functions for the spatial distribution of nonlinear potential (i.e. $\sim I^2$) that we evaluate analytically at the perimeter of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, we describe a new measurement and analysis scheme for angle-resolved transport in the nonlinear regime, which gives rise to full identification of the nonlinear conductivity tensor and helps us uncover the presence of a giant nonlinear Hall effect in Bernal bilayer graphene.

(b) Example of the angle dependence of linear voltage response $V_{\parallel}$ (top) and $V_{\perp}$ (bottom) of an anisotropic linear tensor. (c-e) Nonlinear electrical potential from the $(E_x - iE_y)^2$ (c), $ (E_x+iE_y)^2$ (d), and $E_x^2+E_y^2$ (e) basis functions with current source located at $\phi=0$ (left column), $\phi=\pi/2$ (middle column), and $\phi=7\pi/6$ (right column). (f-g) Angle dependence of the nonlinear voltage response $V_\parallel$ and $V_\perp$ of each basis function: $(E_x - iE_y)^2$ (f), $ (E_x+iE_y)^2$ (g), and $E_x^2+E_y^2$ (h). For all potential maps, current source and drain are indicated by the red arrows, $V_{\parallel}$ A-B contacts are indicated by blue and red circles respectively, and $V_{\perp}$ A-B contacts are indicated by cyan and orange circles respectively. } \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-041: Conductivity tensors and their properties in 2D To better understand the behavior of the nonlinear conductivity tensor under rotations and reflections it is convenient to rewrite Eq. \eqref{Ohms_law in the complex notation as \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j_x + i j_y = \Xi^{(1)}_+ (E_x + i E_y) + \Xi^{(1)}_- (E_x - i E_y) + + \Xi^{(2)}_- (E_x - i E_y)^2 + \Xi^{(2)}_+ (E_x + i E_y)^2 + \Xi^{(2)}_0 \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right). \label{Ohms_law_complex} \end{gathered}\end{equation} The complex parameters $\Xi^{(a) _{b}$ are directly related to the components of the conductivity tensors, see Supplementary Information for explicit formulas.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j_x + i j_y = \Xi^{(1)}_+ (E_x + i E_y) + \Xi^{(1)}_- (E_x - i E_y) + + \Xi^{(2)}_- (E_x - i E_y)^2 + \Xi^{(2)}_+ (E_x + i E_y)^2 + \Xi^{(2)}_0 \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right). \label{Ohms_law_complex} \end{gathered}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-043: On the other hand, both $\Xi^{(2) _+$ and $\Xi^{(2) _0$ terms completely break a rotation symmetry, and therefore must vanish in the presence of such symmetry.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Under the mirror reflection about the $xz$ plane, $M_y$, the $y-$ component of a polar vector changes sign, which is equivalent to complex conjugating the complex vector.
elements.sent-2411-045: Therefore, if $M_y$ is a symmetry, $\Xi^{(2) _b$ must be purely real.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We can understand the consequences of a mirror reflection about an arbitrary (vertical) plane by first rotating by an angle $\alpha$ and then applying $M_y$ in the new coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-047: If such a combined operation is a symmetry, then $e^{-3i\alpha}\Xi^{(2) _-$, $e^{i\alpha}\Xi^{(2) _+$ and $e^{-i\alpha}\Xi^{(2) _0$ must each be purely real.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We will return to this result later in the text.
elements.sent-2411-049: Extracting $\Xi^{(2) _b$ from the experimental data thus offers valuable information about rotational and mirror symmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In order to separate the components of the tensor into dissipative and non-dissipative, we note that the non-dissipative components lead to current without generating any heat.
elements.sent-2411-052: By demanding that the total current density $j^{\text{nd }$ is perpendicular to $E$ for {\em any in-plane orientation of $E$, we readily find that the most general form of the non-dissipative current is $j^{\text{nd = \sigma_H E\times \hat{\bz + \hat{\bz}\timesE \left( \mathcal{C \cdot E \right)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The real vector $\mathcal{C}$ is related to the complex parameters by $i \mathcal{C _x + \mathcal{C _y= \Xi^{(2) _{+ -{\Xi_0^{(2) ^\ast$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-061: Nonlinear transport in disk geometry: experimental setup and an electrostatics problem To study nonlinear transport in BBG we prepare a disk-shaped sample with 8 leads attached to it, where every lead can serve as source/drain for current or measurement point for voltage, see Fig. \ref{fig1 a.}
elements.sent-2411-062: We employ an experimental setup in which the current $I$ is injected at source (S) and removed at drain (D) while the potential difference between two leads A and B is measured.
elements.sent-2411-063: Inversion symmetry in this setup is explicitly broken by an applied perpendicular displacement field from top and bottom gates, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig1 b.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In general, current-voltage characteristic of such setup is nonlinear.
elements.sent-2411-065: For small magnitude of injected current $I$, the measured linear and nonlinear potentials are proportional to $I$ and $I^2$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: To analyze the measurements we make the assumption of local and uniform conductivity tensors (see Fig. 2 in the SI) and map the nonlinear transport problem to an electrostatics problem of potential distribution across the disk sample.
elements.sent-2411-067: To this end, we expand the current density and electric field in powers of $I$ as $\bj=\bj^{(1) +\bj^{(2) +\ldots$ and $\bE=-\nabla \Phi^{(1) -\nabla\Phi^{(2) +\ldots$, and substitute this expansion in the Eq. \ref{Ohms_law .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: To linear order in $I$ we recover the Ohm's law $j^{(1) _\alpha=-\sigma_{\alpha\mu}\nabla_\mu \Phi^{(1) (\br)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The total current density $\bj$ satisfies the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \bj = I (f( \br - \br_S) - f( \br - \br_D)),$ where $f$ is the distribution function describing the finite size source and drain, modeled by a box distribution for arc-shaped leads in the experiment.

elements.sent-2411-072: We require that the normal components of both $\bj^{(1)}$ and $\bj^{(2)}$ vanish at the disk boundary.
elements.sent-2411-073: The exact solution for $\Phi^{(1)}$ for an arbitrary $\sigma_{\alpha\mu}$ is presented in the Ref. \cite{Oskar_solo .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Fitting our experimental data to this solution, we find that $\sigma_H=0$, i.e. there is no anomalous Hall effect.
elements.sent-2411-075: Focusing on $\sigma_H=0$, the vanishing normal components of $\bj$ at the boundary specify the normal derivative of the potential $\Phi^{(1)}$, leading to Neumann boundary conditions \cite{jackson2021classical .}

elements.sent-2411-078: Our analysis of the experimental data shows that the anisotropy of $\sigma_{\alpha\mu}$ is $<5\%$ (in addition to, as mentioned, $\sigma_{H =0$).}

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{fig3v16.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Extraction of the nonlinear conductivity tensor.}} (a) Nonlinear voltage measurement (black circles) versus current source location of 6 different measurement configurations, with 8 different orientations of source per configuration, of the same state and the theoretically expected dependence (black lines) plotted using the extracted nonlinear conductivity tensor. All measurements are performed in a Bernal bilayer graphene sample at $n=-0.15\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$, $D=300$ mV/nm, $T=20$mK. The measurement configurations are indicated as insets. The remarkable agreement between 48 independent measurements and their theoretical fit from the experimentally extracted tensor demonstrates the uniformity of the nonlinear conductivity. (b) Polar plots of $V_{\parallel}$ (top) and $V_{\perp}$ (bottom) signals decomposed into three basis functions using the extracted nonlinear conductivity tensor. (c) The fitted $\Xi^{(2)}_-$, $\Xi^{(2)}_{+}$, and $\Xi^{(2)}_{0}$ plotted in polar coordinates. The shaded cones indicate the fitting uncertainties in angles. The extracted parameters read $\Xi^{(2)}_- = 0.75 e^{0.31 I \pi} \frac{\mu \text{m}}{\Omega \cdot \text{V}}, \Xi^{(2)}_{+} = 7.9 e^{-0.6 I \pi} \frac{\mu \text{m}}{\Omega \cdot \text{V}}, \Xi^{(2)}_{0} = 14.7 e^{0.63 I \pi} \; \frac{\mu \text{m}}{\Omega \cdot \text{V}}.$ (d) Nonlinear current $\bj^{nl}=\tilde{\mathcal{E}}^{(2)}$ distribution calculated for 180$^{\circ}$ source-drain configuration (bottom) using the full extracted tensor decomposed into the dissipative component and non-dissipative component (top). Shaded cones indicate the fitting uncertainties of angles. Plots of the angular dependence of dissipative (e) and non-dissipative (f) component of $V_{\parallel}$ (top) and $V_\perp$ (bottom). } \label{fig3}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Fig4v15.pdf} \caption{{\bf{$n-D$ dependence of the nonlinear conductivity behavior of Bernal bilayer graphene.} } (a-b) Nonlinear voltage measurement (black circles) versus current source location of 6 different measurement configurations, with 8 different orientations of source per configuration, and the theoretical curve plotted using the experimentally extracted tensor (black lines) at (a) $n=-0.15\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$, $D=-300$ mV/nm and (b) $n=-0.3\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$, $D=300$ mV/nm. All measurements are performed in the same Bernal bilayer graphene sample at $T=20$mK. The measurement configurations are indicated as insets. (c) The vector representation of the extracted $\tilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu \nu}$ for three points in the $n-D$ phase diagram. Length of vectors indicate the magnitude of the corresponding component of $\tilde{\sigma}$. Shaded cones indicate the fitting uncertainties of angles. (d) The orientation of the non-dissipative $\mathcal{C}$-vector extracted in the PIP$_2$ and Sym$_4$ phases for different values of $n$ and $D$. Left panel shows the $n-D$ phase diagram with gray areas corresponding to phase transitions as reported in \cite{Andrea_BBG_Science2022}. Black bar indicates the region in the $n-D$ plane where the nonlinear signal changes its orientation by 180$^{\circ}$. Blue and red circles indicate values of $n$ and $D$ within Sym$_4$ and PIP$_2$ phases at which we check orientation of the $\mathcal{C}$-vector. The orientation of the $\mathcal{C}$-vector is robust within the phases as we show in the right panel. } \label{D300Transition}
elements.sent-2411-095: Extraction and analysis of nonlinear conductivity tensor in Bernal bilayer graphene We apply our newly-developed methodology to study nonlinear electron transport in BBG.
elements.sent-2411-096: The optical image and the schematic sketch of the device are shown in Fig. \ref{fig1 a,b.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Top and bottom electrostatic gates allow us to control both electron density, $n$, and the out-of-plane displacement field, $D$.
elements.sent-2411-098: We focus our attention on the region of the phase diagram where the cascade of electronic transitions into correlated metallic states was previously observed in measurements of electron compressibility \cite{Andrea_BBG_Science2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, we study the region that spans partially isospin-polarized phase with 2 large Fermi surfaces (PIP$_2$) and the phase where 4 large Fermi surfaces (FSs) are present, where spin-valley symmetry is believed to be restored (Sym$_4$).
elements.sent-2411-100: First, we analyze linear potential difference and extract $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$ following \cite{Oskar_solo,Zhang2022electronic .}
elements.sent-2411-101: We find that linear conductivity is weakly anisotropic with the anisotropy $<5 \%$ and that $\sigma_H \simeq 0$ across our area of interest in the $n-D$ plane.
elements.sent-2411-102: This indicates that within our experimental accuracy $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$ is isotropic and no anomalous Hall contribution is present.
elements.sent-2411-103: To analyze nonlinear signal we apply slowly varying ($\sim 10$ Hz) AC current and measure potential difference between two leads A, B at the second harmonic frequency.
elements.sent-2411-104: First, we verify that nonlinear potential is $\propto I^2$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Then, we perform measurements of nonlinear potential using measurement configurations that differ in locations of source, drain, A, and B.
elements.sent-2411-106: A representative set of measured nonlinear potential data at $D=300$mV/nm and $n=-0.15\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$ (PIP$_2$ phase) for 6 different configurations is shown in Fig. \ref{fig3 a as a function of source angle as defined in Fig. \ref{fig1 c.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: We apply our methodology, discussed in SI, to extract 3 complex parameters $\Xi^{(2) _{-,+,0}$ (6 real parameters), which we graphically represent in Fig. \ref{fig3 b in the complex plane, out of 48 independent measurement configurations.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The extracted parameters allow us to plot the expected nonlinear voltage drop for every measurement configuration and for any source angle.
elements.sent-2411-109: The expected voltage drop for every configuration is shown in Fig. \ref{fig3 a and it fits the data very well.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Using the extracted $\Xi^{(2) _{-,+,0}$ parameters we find the components of $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ and express them via $\mathcal{A ,\mathcal{B ,\mathcal{C}$ vectors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Interestingly, we find that while rotational symmetry appears intact in linear response, it is manifestly broken in nonlinear response as 3-fold contribution from the $\mathcal{A}$-vector is very small compared to contributions from $\mathcal{B ,\mathcal{C}$-vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-112: In addition, we observe a strong nonlinear Hall signal which is consistent with a presence of a single mirror plane since extracted $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$ vectors are orthogonal within experimental accuracy as we show in Figs.
elements.sent-2411-113: The magnitudes of the nonlinear conductivity components in the PIP$_2$ phase are $\left| \mathcal{A ^3 \right| = 0.75 \pm 0.65 \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{B \right| = 22.5 \pm 3.75\frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{C \right| = 6.88 \pm 2.05\; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V }$ , which is about $100 \%$ larger than the recently observed giant nonlinear conductivity in moir\'{e graphene superlattices \cite{He2022 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In Fig. \ref{fig3 d we show calculated nonlinear current patterns across the disk sample from dissipative and nondissipative contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-115: As we show in the Figs.
elements.sent-2411-116: To gain more insights into possible origins of nonlinear conductivity, especially the $\mathcal{B ,\mathcal{C}$ vectors, we study nonlinear transport across the transition from PIP$_2$ to Sym$_4$ phase.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In Fig. \ref{D300Transition b we show nonlinear signal inside Sym$_4$ phase with $D=300$mV/nm and $n=-0.3\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Unexpectedly, the Sym$_4$ phase exhibits a non-zero response in the nonlinear channel.
elements.sent-2411-119: Like in the PIP$_2$ phase, we utilize the angular fit to extract $\mathcal{A}$, $\mathcal{B}$, and $\mathcal{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-120: We find $\mathcal{A}$ vector to be very small, whereas both $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$ rotated by nearly 180$^{\circ}$, within our experimental accuracy, from the orientation of the same vectors in PIP$_2$, see Fig. \ref{D300Transition c.}
elements.sent-2411-121: By performing angle-resolved measurement with varying $n$ over a wide range of $D$, we find that the $180^{\circ}$ rotation in $\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{C}$ is well-aligned with the transition line extracted from the compressibility measurements \cite{Andrea_BBG_Science2022 (Fig. \ref{D300Transition d).}}
elements.sent-2411-122: That the PIP$_2$ to Sym$_4$ transition prominently manifests itself in nonlinear transport offers a new constraint for understanding the nature of the states on each side of the transition.
elements.sent-2411-123: We also check that the structure of $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ remains nearly the same and $\mathcal{C}$ vector orientation is quite robust within each of the two phases, see Fig. \ref{D300Transition d and SI for all extracted components of the tensor.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Interestingly, the magnitude of nonlinear conductivity tensor extracted in Sym$_4$ phase is almost 100 larger than that in PIP$_2$ phase with $\left| \mathcal{A ^3 \right| = 100 \pm 82.9\frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{B \right| = 2072 \pm 873\frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{C \right| = 668 \pm 339 \; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: To our knowledge, these are the record numbers reported for nonlinear conductivity so far.
elements.sent-2411-126: The larger uncertainty in magnitudes of $\mathcal{A}$, $\mathcal{B}$, and $\mathcal{C}$ is caused by the larger uncertainty in the value of linear conductivity due to proximity of the Sym$_4$ phase to the ballistic regime.
elements.sent-2411-127: Dissipative contribution to nonlinear current has four possible origins: (i) disorder, e.g., skew scattering ~\cite{KoenigDzeroLevchenkoPesin,IsobeSciAdv2020,Du2021NatComm,AtenciaNHE2023 ; (ii) interactions ~\cite{MorimotoNagaosa2018SciReps ; (iii) quantum geometry ~\cite{MichishitaNagaosaPRB2022,AgarwalQG2023PRB,KaplanPRL2024 ; (iv) second-order Drude contributions to $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha\mu \nu}$ \cite{Wang2021PRL,KaplanPRL2024 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: On the other hand, the BCD can naturally account for the non-dissipative component, the nonlinear Hall effect, in the absence of TR breaking.
elements.sent-2411-130: If the BCD alone is responsible for the nonlinear Hall effect, reversing $D$ is expected to influence the orientation of the $\mathcal{C}$-vector ~\cite{Sodemann_Fu .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Alternatively, BCD-induced $\mathcal{C}$-vector could preserve its orientation while $\mathcal{A}$ and $\mathcal{B}$, given by quantum geometric or second-order Drude contributions, reverse sign under $D \rightarrow -D$, given that the order parameter associated with the nematic perturbation is linked to the direction of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-132: An example of this scenario is the charge $E_u$ order parameter, which breaks TR symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-133: We examine these scenarios by extracting $\mathcal{A ,\mathcal{B ,\mathcal{C}$ vectors at $D= \pm 300$mV/nm.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Fig. \ref{D300Transition c shows the angular dependence of nonlinear response taken at $D=-300$mV/nm and $n=-0.15\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The magnitudes are given by $\left| \mathcal{A ^3 \right| = 0.92 \pm 0.58\frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{B \right| = 11.5 \pm 2.14\frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V , \left| \mathcal{C \right| = 4.46 \pm 1.69 \; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V .$ The orientations of both the $\mathcal{C}$-vector and $\mathcal{B}$-vector remain the same as in Fig. \ref{fig3 d.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, the invariance of $\mathcal{B}$-vector and $\mathcal{C}$-vector, combined, indicates that nonlinear Hall effect is not induced by the BCD mechanism as the order parameter associated with the nematic perturbation appears invariant under a sign change in $D$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Moreover, the typical magnitude of nonlinear Hall conductivity associated with BCD is $\sim 10^{-5 \div 10^{-7 \; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V }$ \cite{KinFai2019,Pablo2019,He2022 -- several orders of magnitude smaller than $|\mathcal{C |$ in both PIP$_2$ and Sym$_4$ phases.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Combined, our observations pose a peculiar conundrum: linear conductivity appears to be nearly isotropic while nonlinear conductivity manifestly breaks rotational symmetry, seemingly down to a single mirror plane.
elements.sent-2411-139: The apparent decoupling between the symmetry of linear and nonlinear signal is also reported in a previous observation \cite{JamesLeo2024NatMat .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Notably, rotational symmetry is broken for both the dissipative and nondissipative contributions to nonlinear current, which is evidenced by the angular dependence in Fig.~\ref{fig3 e and f, and for both PIP$_2$ and Sym$_4$ phases.}
elements.sent-2411-141: We also observe that the magnitude of measured nonlinear signal increases with $D$ (see Fig. 6 in SI) ~\cite{Lin2023Arxiv .}
elements.sent-2411-142: According to Fig.~\ref{D300Transition c, our observations in the nonlinear regime exhibit vanishingly small 3-fold contribution.}
elements.sent-2411-143: This rules out skew scattering and side jumps as the origin of nonlinearity, since both produce a large 3-fold contribution to nonlinear current even in the presence of rotational symmetry breaking with magnitude $\sim 1$meV, see SI.
elements.sent-2411-144: A typical value of quantum geometry-induced nonlinear conductivity is $\sim 10^{-4 \div 10^{-5 \; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V }$ \cite{Han2024 , which is orders of magnitude smaller than the extracted values in Fig. \ref{D300Transition .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: On a qualitative level, one can account for the observed phenomena within the second-order Drude contribution to nonlinear conductivity by assuming the presence of a weak spin-orbit coupling and of a weak order parameter that breaks time reversal and rotational symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-146: In such case one can correctly reproduce the hierarchy of $\left| \mathcal{A ^3 \right|, \left| \mathcal{B \right|, \left| \mathcal{C \right|$, the presence of a mirror axis, and the reorientation transition of $\mathcal{B , \mathcal{C}$-vectors between PIP$_2$ and Sym$_4$ phases.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: However, as we show in the SI, none of the known microscopic mechanisms can account for the magnitude of the signal as they fall short orders of magnitude, e.g., the skew scattering which was estimated to induce $\tilde{\sigma \lesssim 0.01 \; \frac{\mu \text{m {\Omega \cdot \text{V }$ \cite{IsobeSciAdv2020,He2022 in graphene-based systems.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Overall, the observed structures and magnitudes of linear and nonlinear conductivity tensors in PIP$_2$ and Sym$_4$ phases, the existence of the reorientation transition of nonlinear signal between the two phases, and the mean free path restriction (less than the diameter of the disk in the diffusive regime) put severe constraints which cannot be satisfied by any known microscopic mechanism of nonlinear transport.
elements.sent-2411-149: Discussion and outlook Here we presented a systematic way of analyzing nonlinear transport measurements and unambiguously extracting nonlinear conductivity tensor from experimental data, which sets up the foundation for all future experimental works in the vibrant field of nonlinear electron transport.
elements.sent-2411-150: We used our approach to determine nonlinear conductivity tensor in Bernal bilayer graphene and show the presence of giant nonlinear Hall effect.
elements.sent-2411-151: We also found that nonlinear signal strongly breaks rotational symmetry whereas in the linear regime this symmetry appears intact.
elements.sent-2411-152: The new way of extracting nonlinear conductivity tensor allowed us to conclude that none of the known microscopic mechanisms for nonlinear transport can account for the structure and/or magnitude of the measured nonlinear conductivity tensor.
elements.sent-2411-153: The methodology presented in this work allows to systematically study nonlinear transport of quasi-2D materials \cite{Cao2018,Yankowitz_Science_2019,Wong2020,Zondiner2020,Choi2021,Park2021,Kim2022,Liu2022,Zhou2021,Andrea_BBG_Science2022,Zhang2023,Zeng2023_FCI,Park2023_FCI,Lu2024_FCI,Fan_Xu_PRX_FCI,TelluriumPRL2024 and paves the way to future discoveries.}

elements.sent-2411-157: Isolating the second order response from the dominating first order part using a DC current source can be experimentally tedious.
elements.sent-2411-158: One would need to first measure the total voltage response as a function of current, then remove the first order component by performing a linear fit at $I_{DC =0$ (See Fig.~\ref{figSI_IV a).}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Experimentally we measure the second harmonic response of the injected AC current (while keeping the frequency around $13$Hz to be at DC limit) to extract the second order current response.
elements.sent-2411-160: This can be understood easily by plugging $I_{AC =I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ into $V=aI + bI^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-161: With some trigonometry, one can see that the second order term gives raise to a signal with frequency $2\omega$, which can be easily isolated out with the second harmonic function on a lock-in amplifier.
elements.sent-2411-162: As shown Fig.~\ref{figSI_IV b, the isolated second harmonic signal follows a quadratic relation with respect to the amplitude of the AC current and is a factor of 2 smaller than DC measurement from the trigonometry relation.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Just like the pure DC measurement, the second harmonic measurement can pickup contributions from higher order terms.
elements.sent-2411-164: To ensure that we are working within the current limit where the quadratic term is the only non-vanishing nonlinear term (the quadratic regime), we measure the second harmonic signal as a function of $I_0$, the amplitude of the injected AC bias (See Fig.~\ref{figSI_IV c top panel).}
elements.sent-2411-165: Then we find the largest $I_0$ below which the relation $V^{2\omega \propto I_0^2$ still holds (black straight line when plotting in power scale).}
elements.sent-2411-166: Such cutoff ($60$nA in this example), is indicated by vanishing residual below $I_0$ and a sharp onset above $I_0$ (Fig.~\ref{figSI_IV c bottom panel).}
elements.sent-2411-167: Working at a current below the cutoff ($40$nA vs $60$nA in this work) ensures the quadratic regime requirement is fulfilled.
elements.sent-2411-168: The sample consists of a Bernal bilayer graphene dual-encapsulated by top and bottom graphite gates, separated with hexagonal BN.
elements.sent-2411-169: The stack is picked up with a standard poly(bisphenol A carbonate) (PC)/polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) stamp on a glass slide.
elements.sent-2411-170: The stack is then released on to a doped Si/Si)${ _2$ substrate and patterned using electron beam lithography with a minimum resolution of about $100$nm, yielding a contact with angular width of $18^\circ$ for a disk of diameter $2\mu$m.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Metal contacts are then made with Cr/Au (2nm/100nm respectively).
elements.sent-2411-172: The dual-gate structure allows separate control over charge carrier density and displacement field.
elements.sent-2411-173: Transport measurements are performed with Standford Research 860 and 830 Lock-in amplifiers at about 13Hz, in a BlueFors LD400 dilution refrigerator with a base temperature of ~20mK.
elements.sent-2411-174: The fast configuration switching is allowed by Keysight 34980A data acquisition system and a custom designed breakout box.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figSI_IV_v2.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Second harmonic measurement and quadratic regime} } (a) An exmaple $I-V$ relation, illustrating how second order voltage response is extracted using DC measurement. (b) Assuming the same $I-V$ relation, using second harmonic AC measurement to extract second order voltage response. (c) Experimental data of $V^{2\omega}$ as a function of the square of AC current amplitude $I_{AC}^2$, showing how quadratic regime is determined. } \label{figSI_IV}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.55\linewidth]{FigFull.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Angle-resolved transport in the linear regime.} } Transport response in the linear regime was measured using six different configurations, with eight distinct orientations of current flow per configuration. Solid black lines represent the expected transport response derived from a single conductivity matrix in the linear regime, while the gray shaded stripes denote the anticipated measurement error due to the finite width of the graphene leads. Notably, certain configurations probe the transport response near the corners of the disk-shaped sample, such as the configuration depicted in the bottom left panel. The consistent fit of all measurements to the same conductivity matrix strongly supports the conclusion that the transport response is both local and uniform across the sample. } \label{full_linear}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figSI_linear_v4.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Linear full fit} } Full fit for extracting the linear conductivity matrix for (a-c) $n=-0.15 \times 10^{12}$cm${}^{-2}$ and $D=300$ mV/nm, (d-f) $n=-0.15 \times 10^{12}$cm${}^{-2}$ and $D=-300$ mV/nm, and (g-i) $n=-0.3 \times 10^{12}$cm${}^{-2}$ and $D=300$ mV/nm. Panel (a,d,g) show the fitting result (black curves) compared against measured data (black open circles) over 48 difference configurations, allowing anisotropy and Hall conductance. All six lines in each panel comes from the same linear conductivity matrix. Grey shades in denotes the error bar of the fit from the finite width of the sample contacts. In each case $\Delta\sigma / \bar{\sigma} \approx 3\%$, and $\sigma_{H} \simeq 0$. In PIP$_2$ phase linear conductivity is $\bar{\sigma} \simeq 41 \frac{e^2}{h} $ while in Sym$_4$ phase $\bar{\sigma} \simeq 298 \frac{e^2}{h} $. Panel (b,e,h) show the fitting result (black curves) compared against measured data (black open circles) over 48 difference configurations, forcing $\Delta\sigma / \bar{\sigma} = 0$, and $\sigma_{H} = 0$. Contrasting (a,d,g) with (g,e,h), we demonstrate the anisotropy and Hall conductance in each state is weak. (d,f,i) shows how $RRMSE$ \cite{JamesLeo2024NatMat} changes as we sweep different parameters of the linear conductivity matrix. Left panels shows a sharp dip at $\bar{\sigma}$, indicating an precise extraction of $\bar{\sigma}$. Middle panels shows a flat response as we sweep the principle axis angle. The insensitivity confirms that the anisotropy is weak (see section Fitting Protocol and Error Estimation). Right panel shows a sharp dip near 0, confirming a small $\sigma_H$ compared to $\bar{\sigma}$. } \label{figSI_linear}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{FigSI_nD3vec.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Full fit in $n-D$ phase space. } } (a) Same $n-D$ phase diagram as shown in Fig. 4d of the main text, with all three fitted vectors plotted on the right hand side. The green triangle depicts $\mathcal{A}^3$, red arrow shows $\mathcal{B}$, and blue arrow indicates $\mathcal{C}$. The size of the triangle and the arrows indicates relative magnitude of different components of the tensor, normalized to each corresponding $\mathcal{B}$ vector. Shaded cones indicate the fitting uncertainties of angles. The larger 3-fold component in the Sym$_4$ phase likely is caused by measurement uncertainty due to lower signal-to-noise ratio in the Sym$_4$ phase. } \label{figSI_nD3vec}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figSI_QO.pdf} \caption{ {\bf{Quantum oscillations data for Sym${}_\mathbf{4}$ state.} } (a) $n-B$ map at $D=300$mV/nm. (b) FFT of panel (a). The peak near $1/4$ at $n=-0.3\times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$ indicates four nearly degenerate Fermi pockets. } \label{figSI_QO}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figSI_Dv3.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Displacement field dependence of the second harmonic response. } } (a-b) Displacement field dependence of the second harmonic response amplitude measured with difference configuration, at (a) $n=-0.15 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$ and (b) $n=-0.1 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$. (c-d) Displacement field dependence of the second harmonic response measured with difference configuration, at (c) $n=-0.02 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$ and (d) $n=0.04 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$. } \label{figSI_D}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{figSI_temp.pdf} \caption{{\bf{Temperature dependence of the second harmonic response. } } (a) $n-T$ map at $D=150$mV/nm. (b-c) Temperature dependence of the second harmonic response measured with difference configuration, at (b) $n=-0.15 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$ and (c) $n=-0.1 \times 10^{12}$ cm$^{-2}$.} \label{figSI_temperature}
elements.section-2411-002: Ohm's law and the symmetries of linear and nonlinear conductivity tensors
elements.sent-2411-175: Ohm's law relates current $j$ generated in media to the applied electric field $E$.
elements.sent-2411-176: In the standard linear case $j$ depends linearly on $E$.
elements.sent-2411-178: In 2 spatial dimensions linear conductivity tensor $\sigma_{\alpha \mu}$ is a $2 \times 2$ matrix, which in the absence of any symmetries has 4 independent components.
elements.sent-2411-179: This statement is valid for both AC and DC currents.
elements.sent-2411-180: The structure of nonlinear conductivity tensor does depend on whether the applied electric field is time-dependent or not.
elements.sent-2411-181: In the case of AC field and in the absence of any spatial symmetries, $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ can have up to 8 independent components in 2 spatial dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-182: In the DC case, $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ has to be symmetric with respect to $\mu \leftrightarrow \nu$, therefore, the maximal number of independent components is reduced to 6.}
elements.sent-2411-183: We restrict our studies solely to the DC case in 2 spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-184: In coordinate representation linear and nonlinear tensors can be written in terms of Pauli matrices \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \sigma_{\alpha \mu} = \mathfrak{k}_0 \tau_0 + \mathfrak{k}_1 \tau_1 + i \mathfrak{k}_2 \tau_2 + \mathfrak{k}_3 \tau_3 \tilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu \nu} = \mathfrak{c}_0^{\alpha} \tau_0 + \mathfrak{c}_1^{\alpha} \tau_1 + \mathfrak{c}_3^{\alpha} \tau_3, \end{gathered}\end{equation} where $\mathfrak{c _2^{\alpha \tau_2$ component is absent in the DC limit.}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \sigma_{\alpha \mu} = k_0 \tau_0 + k_1 \tau_1 + i k_2 \tau_2 + k_3 \tau_3 \tilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu \nu} = c_0^{\alpha} \tau_0 + c_1^{\alpha} \tau_1 + c_3^{\alpha} \tau_3, \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-185: Spatial (point-group) symmetries set constraints on the structure of conductivity tensors.
elements.sent-2411-186:

u}$ remain invariant, i.e.,  egin{equation} egin{gathered} R_{\alpha' \alpha} \sigma_{\alpha \mu} (R^{-1})_{\mu \mu'} = \sigma_{\alpha' \mu'} = \sigma_{\alpha \mu}, R_{\alpha' \alpha} 	ilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu 
u} (R^{-1})_{\mu \mu'} (R^{-1})_{

u'} = 	ilde{\sigma}_{\alpha' \mu' 
u'} = 	ilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu 
u}, \end{gathered} \label{C3_tensor_transformation}\end{equation} where $R$ -- is the matrix of rotation by $2 \pi /3$.}
elements.sent-2411-187: By solving Eq. \eqref{C3_tensor_transformation for coefficients $\mathfrak{k _j$ and $\mathfrak{c _j^{\alpha}$ with requirement that all coefficients are real we arrive at the constraint for linear tensor \begin{equation}\mathfrak{k}_1 = \mathfrak{k}_3 =0\end{equation} and for nonlinear tensor \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \mathfrak{c}_1^2 = -\mathfrak{c}_3^1, \; \; \; \mathfrak{c}_3^2 = \mathfrak{c}_1^1, \end{gathered}\end{equation} and all other coefficients are zeros.}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Equation \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate can also be conveniently written in the complex form, where 2D vectors are treated as complex numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-189: For the purely linear case Ohm's law in the complex form reads \begin{equation}j_x^{l} + i j_y^{l} = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}j_x^{l} + i j_y^{l} = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-190: The four real parameters $\sigma^{(1) _{+ , \sigma^{(1) _{- , \chi^{(1) _{+ , \chi^{(1) _{-}$ in complex representation can be easily related to the four real parameters $\mathfrak{k _{0 , \mathfrak{k _1, \mathfrak{k _2, \mathfrak{k _3$ of real representation: \begin{eqnarray} \mathfrak{k}_0 = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} \cos \chi^{(1)}_{+}, \mathfrak{k}_3 = \sigma^{(1)}_{-} \cos \chi^{(1)}_{-}, \mathfrak{k}_1 = \sigma^{(1)}_{-} \sin \chi^{(1)}_{-}, \mathfrak{k}_2 = - \sigma^{(1)}_{+} \sin \chi^{(1)}_{+}.\end{eqnarray}.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Let us now focus on the nonlinear contribution to the electric current in Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate .}
elements.sent-2411-192: There are two ways to derive the complex form of the nonlinear contribution.
elements.sent-2411-193: One is to explicitly use the coordinate representation and match coefficients in front of equivalent terms.
elements.sent-2411-194: The other one involves an analogy with the multiplication of two-dimensional irreducible representations (irreps) of point groups.
elements.sent-2411-195: Let us use the more elegant method of multiplying irreps.
elements.sent-2411-196: The right-hand side of the equation can be thought of as a product of two $E$ irreducible representations (because electric field is a 2-component vector).
elements.sent-2411-197: To use this analogy let us recall the irrep multiplication rule $E \times E = A_1 + A_2 + E$.
elements.sent-2411-198: Imagine that we want to calculate a Kronecker product of two different two-component irreps $E$ with basis vectors $(P_x \pm i P_y)$ and $(Q_x \pm i Q_y)$.
elements.sent-2411-199: Then the $A_1$ combination is $P_x Q_x + P_y Q_y$ (which comes from $(P_x + i P_y)(Q_x - i Q_y) + c.c.$); two $E$ combinations are $P_x Q_x - P_y Q_y$ and $P_x Q_y + P_y Q_x$ (both come from linear combinations of $(P_x + i P_y)(Q_x + i Q_y)$ and $(P_x - i P_y)(Q_x - i Q_y)$); and the $A_2$ combination is $P_x Q_y - P_y Q_x$ (this one comes from $(P_x + i P_y)(Q_x - i Q_y) - c.c.$).
elements.sent-2411-200: We now use the fact that in our case both $P-$ and $Q-$ vectors are the time-independent electric field $E$, therefore, the $A_2$ combination identically vanishes and there are no two additional independent parameters that would correspond to the $A_2$ contribution.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Dissipative vs non-dissipative components of conductivity tensor
elements.sent-2411-218: In linear response theory Onsager relations uniquely relate time-reversal symmetry breaking (non-reciprocity) and dissipation.
elements.sent-2411-219: It is well known that Hall component of linear conductivity tensor is non-dissipative and requires broken time-reversal symmetry to be present.
elements.sent-2411-220: The rest of the tensor is dissipative.
elements.sent-2411-221: This can be inferred from Joule-Lenz law applied to current density in linear regime.
elements.sent-2411-222: First, we split the overall current into two parts, which we label dissipative and non-dissipative \begin{equation}j = j^{d} + j^{nd}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-223: This separation is performed for linear and nonlinear contributions to current separately.
elements.sent-2411-224: Non-dissipative components of the tensor are those that lead to current not generating any heat for any orientation of the electric field, i.e., such that \begin{equation}j^{nd} \cdot E = 0. \label{Non-dissipative_criterion}\end{equation} To find the non-dissipative component we calculate the dot product of the full current $j=j^{d + j^{nd}$ with the electric field and look for the contribution that satisfies Eq. \eqref{Non-dissipative_criterion .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: In linear regime, the only component satisfying this condition is $\mathfrak{k _2$, which corresponds to Hall conductivity: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{l} \cdot E = j_{\alpha}^{l} E_{\alpha} = \sigma_{\alpha \mu} E_{\mu} E_{\alpha} = E_{\alpha} (\mathfrak{k}_0 \tau_0 + \mathfrak{k}_1 \tau_1 + i \mathfrak{k}_2 \tau_2 + \mathfrak{k}_3 \tau_3)_{\alpha \mu} E_{\mu} = =\mathfrak{k}_0 \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right) + \mathfrak{k}_3 \left( E_x^2 - E_y^2 \right) + 2 \mathfrak{k}_1 E_x E_y. \end{gathered}\end{equation} The same conclusion can be reached using the complex representation with $E_x + i E_y = |E| e^{i \lambda}$: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{l} \cdot E = \mathrm{Re} \left[ (j_x^{l} + i j_y^{l}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right)^2 \right] = = |E|^2 \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} e^{-2 i \lambda} \right]=0, \end{gathered}\end{equation} where we request the equality to hold for any orientation of the electric field, i.e., for any $\lambda$.}
elements.formula-2411-026: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{l} \cdot E = j_{\alpha}^{l} E_{\alpha} = \sigma_{\alpha \mu} E_{\mu} E_{\alpha} = E_{\alpha} (k_0 \tau_0 + k_1 \tau_1 + i k_2 \tau_2 + k_3 \tau_3)_{\alpha \mu} E_{\mu} = =k_0 \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right) + k_3 \left( E_x^2 - E_y^2 \right) + 2 k_1 E_x E_y. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{l} \cdot E = Re \left[ (j_x^{l} + i j_y^{l}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = Re \left[ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right)^2 \right] = = |E|^2 Re \left[ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} e^{-2 i \lambda} \right]=0, \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore, the only component of the current that satisfies this condition is non-dissipative component \begin{equation}\left(j^{nd}_x + i j^{nd}_y\right)^l = \frac{ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} }{2} \left( E_x + i E_y \right)\end{equation} which is exactly the linear Hall component, and the rest provides the linear dissipative part: \begin{equation}\left( j^{d}_x + i j^{d}_y \right)^l = \frac{ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} }{2} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}\left(j^{nd}_x + i j^{nd}_y\right)^l = \frac{ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} }{2} \left( E_x + i E_y \right)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\left( j^{d}_x + i j^{d}_y \right)^l = \frac{ \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} }{2} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-227: For nonlinear component of the current the situation is more complicated.
elements.sent-2411-228: There is no consensus on Onsager relations for nonlinear conductivity tensor; this is a topic of an extensive ongoing research.
elements.sent-2411-229: Therefore, we adopt the Joule-Lenz criterion \eqref{Non-dissipative_criterion for microscopic current and apply it directly to the nonlinear contribution to current density in complex representation with $E_x + i E_y = |E| e^{i \lambda}$: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{nl} \cdot E = \mathrm{Re} \left[ (j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = = \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}}(E_x-iE_y)^3 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)(E_x^2+E_y^2) + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} (E_x-iE_y) \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right) \right] = = |E|^3 \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \lambda} \right] = 0. \end{gathered}\end{equation} For the non-dissipative contribution, this condition has to be satisfied for any $\lambda$, hence \begin{eqnarray}\sigma^{(2)}_{-} \cos \left( \chi^{(2)}_{-} - 3 \lambda \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} \cos \left( \chi^{(2)}_{+} + \lambda \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} \cos \left( \chi^{(2)}_{0} - \lambda \right) = 0.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{nl} \cdot E = Re \left[ (j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = = Re \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}}(E_x-iE_y)^3 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)(E_x^2+E_y^2) + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} (E_x-iE_y) \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right) \right] = = |E|^3 Re \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \lambda} \right] = 0. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-230: Since $\sin n \lambda, \cos m \lambda$ are linearly independent, we obtain that non-dissipative component of the nonlinear tensor requires \begin{eqnarray} \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = 0, \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) + \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0, \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) - \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0. \label{nondis_conds}\end{eqnarray} Thus the full nonlinear current reads \begin{equation}j^{nl} = j^{nl}_d + j^{nl}_{nd},\end{equation} where the non-dissipative component of the current satisfies \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \left(j_x^{nd} + i j_y^{nd} \right)^{nl} = \frac{\sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}}}{2} (E_x + i E_y)^2 - \frac{\sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}}}{2} \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right). \end{gathered} \label{nonlin_nondiss}\end{equation} and the rest of the nonlinear tensor corresponds to the dissipative component of the nonlinear current and is given by \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \left(j^{d}_{x} + i j^{d}_{y}\right)^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - i E_y)^2 + \frac{\sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}}}{2} (E_x + i E_y)^2 + \frac{\sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}}}{2} \left( E_x^2 + E_y^2 \right). \end{gathered}\end{equation} Note that Eq. \eqref{nonlin_nondiss can be written as \begin{equation}\left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot j^{nl}_{nd} = \left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot \left(\hat{z} \times E \right) \left( \mathcal{C} \cdot E \right),\ \label{nonlin_nondiss_Liang_form}\end{equation} where $E$ -- is the electric field, $\hat{x ,\hat{y ,\hat{z}$ -- are unit vectors in $x,y$ and $z$ (out-of-plane) directions correspondingly, and a 2-component vector $\mathcal{C}$ is given by \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \mathcal{C}_x = \mathrm{Im} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}}\right], \mathcal{C}_y = \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right]. \end{gathered}\end{equation} Note that Eq. \eqref{nonlin_nondiss_Liang_form becomes exactly the Berry curvature dipole-induced nonlinear Hall effect if $\mathcal{C = \mathcal{D}$ -- is the Berry curvature dipole vector \cite{Sodemann_Fu .}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-233: It is important to mention that 3-fold contribution $\sigma^{(2) _{- e^{i \chi^{(2) _{- (E_x - i E_y)^2$ does not contribute to the nonlinear Hall and is different from the 1-fold dissipative contribution: it always contains both transverse and longitudinal contributions and neither of them can be made vanishing throughout the entire sample, see next section.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: The other condition that can be easily obtained from the complex representation of Ohm's law is the condition on nonlinear component of the current to always be longitudinal to the applied electric field (no transverse component).
elements.sent-2411-235: For microscopic current density and electric field it is given by \begin{equation}j^{nl} \times E = 0,\end{equation} which for nonlinear contribution to current in complex representation translates to \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{nl} \times E = -\mathrm{Im} \left[ (j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = -|E|^3 \mathrm{Im} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \lambda} \right] = 0. \end{gathered}\end{equation} Then for any $\lambda$ \begin{eqnarray}\sigma^{(2)}_{-} \sin \left( \chi^{(2)}_{-} - 3 \lambda \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} \sin \left( \chi^{(2)}_{+} + \lambda \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} \sin \left( \chi^{(2)}_{0} - \lambda \right) = 0.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-039: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} j^{nl} \times E = -Im \left[ (j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl}) (E_x - i E_y) \right] = -|E|^3 Im \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \lambda} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \lambda} \right] = 0. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-236: Again, $\sin n \lambda, \cos m \lambda$ are linearly independent, therefore, the condition is satisfied for any $\lambda$ if \begin{eqnarray} \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = 0, \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) - \mathrm{Re} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0, \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) + \mathrm{Im} \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}Re \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = Im \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} \right) = 0, Re \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) - Re \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0, Im \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \right) + Im \left( \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right) = 0.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-237: As we see from those conditions, the 3-fold component cannot be made purely longitudinal as it always will have transverse components in addition to longitudinal.
elements.sent-2411-238: The 1-fold (longitudinal) component of dissipative nonlinear current can also be cast into the coordinate-free way similar to Eq. \eqref{nonlin_nondiss_Liang_form so that the full non-dissipative nonlinear current satisfies \begin{equation}\left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot j^{nl}_{d} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - i E_y)^2 + \left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot E \left( \mathcal{B} \cdot E \right)\end{equation} upon the introduction of an auxiliary vector $\mathcal{B}$, components of which are given by \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \mathcal{B}_x = \mathrm{Re} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right], \mathcal{B}_y = \mathrm{Im} \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right]. \end{gathered}\end{equation} The relative orientation of $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{B}$ vectors can be inferred from their dot product \begin{equation}\mathcal{B} \cdot \mathcal{C} = 2 \sigma^{(2)}_{+} \sigma^{(2)}_{0} \sin \left( \chi^{(2)}_{+} + \chi^{(2)}_{0} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation}\left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot j^{nl}_{d} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - i E_y)^2 + \left( \hat{x} + i \hat{y} \right) \cdot E \left( B \cdot E \right)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-043: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} B_x = Re \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right], B_y = Im \left[ \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \right]. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-239: In the presence of mirror axis $\chi^{(2) _{+ = - \chi^{(2) _{0}$ the dot product vanishes indicating that the two vectors are orthogonal.}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: In the absence of mirror axis the two vectors can have arbitrary orientation.
elements.sent-2411-241: Carthesian and vector representations can be linked using the definitions of $\mathcal{A , \mathcal{B , \mathcal{C}$ and Eq. \eqref{Complex_Carthesian_1 : \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \tilde{\sigma}_{xxx} = \mathcal{A}_x^3 - 3 \mathcal{A}_x \mathcal{A}_y^2 + \mathcal{B}_x, \tilde{\sigma}_{xxy} = 3 \mathcal{A}_x^2 \mathcal{A}_y - \mathcal{A}_y^3 + \frac{\mathcal{B}_y - \mathcal{C}_x}{2}, \tilde{\sigma}_{xyy} = - \left( \mathcal{A}_x^3 - 3 \mathcal{A}_x \mathcal{A}_y^2 \right) - \mathcal{C}_y, \tilde{\sigma}_{yxx} = 3 \mathcal{A}_x^2 \mathcal{A}_y - \mathcal{A}_y^3 + \mathcal{C}_x, \tilde{\sigma}_{yxy} = - \left( \mathcal{A}_x^3 - 3 \mathcal{A}_x \mathcal{A}_y^2 \right) + \frac{\mathcal{B}_x + \mathcal{C}_y}{2}, \tilde{\sigma}_{yyy} = -\left( 3 \mathcal{A}_x^2 \mathcal{A}_y - \mathcal{A}_y^3 \right) + \mathcal{B}_y. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Symmetry properties of nonlinear conductivity tensor
elements.sent-2411-242: Nonlinear conductivity tensor has a higher rank than linear conductivity tensor, therefore, its transformation properties are less obvious.
elements.sent-2411-243: In this section we discuss symmetry constraints on the components of nonlinear conductivity tensor.
elements.sent-2411-244: The most fundamental symmetry constraint on the nonlinear conductivity tensor is the absence of inversion symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-245: The absence of inversion is evident from Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate : in order to have nonzero linear response the nonlinear tensor $\tilde{\sigma _{\alpha \mu \nu}$ has to be odd under inversion to be nonzero because both $j$ and $E$ vectors are odd under inversion.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: In this regard nonlinear conductivity tensor is very similar to the optical second-order nonlinear susceptibility, which is responsible for generation of second optical harmonic in noncentrosymmetric materials or from the surface of centrosymmetric materials.
elements.sent-2411-247: We start the discussion of point-group symmetries by considering the effects of rotation.
elements.sent-2411-248: We focus solely on nonlinear contribution to current density and, for convenience, resort to the complex representation \eqref{Ohm_in_complex .}
elements.sent-2411-249: In complex representation rotation of vectors by angle $\alpha$ is performed by a simple multiplication by a phase factor: \begin{equation}v_x \pm i v_y \rightarrow e^{\pm i \alpha} ( v_x \pm i v_y ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-046: \begin{equation}v_x \pm i v_y \rightarrow e^{\pm i \alpha} ( v_x \pm i v_y ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-250: Then under rotation nonlinear component of Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_complex transforms as \begin{equation}e^{i \alpha} ( j_x + i j_y )^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-2 i \alpha} (E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{2 i \alpha} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-047: \begin{equation}e^{i \alpha} ( j_x + i j_y )^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-2 i \alpha} (E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{2 i \alpha} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-251: Dividing both sides of the equation by $e^{i \alpha}$ we arrive at \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \alpha} (E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \alpha} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \alpha} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} e^{-3 i \alpha} (E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} e^{i \alpha} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} e^{-i \alpha} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-252: Rotational symmetry requires the prefactors containing $\alpha$ to vanish.
elements.sent-2411-253: The first term ($\sigma^{(2) _{-}$-term), therefore, is invariant upon $C_3$ rotation, as $e^{- 3 i \alpha = 1$ for $\alpha = \pm \frac{2\pi {3}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: The other two terms, $\sigma^{(2) _{+}$ and $\sigma^{(2) _{0}$ are only invariant upon the full $2 \pi$ rotation.}}
elements.sent-2411-255: All three terms break $C_{2z}$ (in-plane inversion) symmetry as they change sign for $\alpha = \pi$.
elements.sent-2411-256: Another important point-group symmetry is mirror.
elements.sent-2411-257: In 2D there are two generic mirror symmetries, $M_x$ and $M_y$, that act as \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M_x: x \rightarrow x, \; \; \; y \rightarrow - y, M_y: x \rightarrow -x, \; \; \; y \rightarrow y. \end{gathered}\end{equation} Under $M_x$ all complex vectors in Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_complex experience complex conjugation: \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} - i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M_x: x \rightarrow x, \; \; \; y \rightarrow - y, M_y: x \rightarrow -x, \; \; \; y \rightarrow y. \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-050: \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} - i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-258: Applying complex conjugation to the whole expression we obtain \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{-i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-051: \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{-i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, the presence of $M_x$ symmetry requires all phase factors $\chi^{(2) _{- , \chi^{(2) _{+ , \chi^{(2) _{0}$ to be $0$ or $\pi$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Under $M_y$ Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_complex becomes \begin{equation}- j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}- j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-261: Applying complex conjugation to the whole equation and multiplying both sides by -1 we arrive at \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = -\sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{-i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 - \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}j_x^{nl} + i j_y^{nl} = -\sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{-i\chi^{(2)}_{-}} (E_x - iE_y)^2 - \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x + iE_y)^2 - \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{-i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-262: Therefore, to preserve invariance one has to satisfy $-e^{-i\chi^{(2) _{- =e^{i\chi^{(2) _{- ; -e^{-i\chi^{(2) _{+ = e^{i\chi^{(2) _{+ ; -e^{-i\chi^{(2) _{0 = e^{i\chi^{(2) _{0 }$, which leads to all phase factors $\chi^{(2) _{- , \chi^{(2) _{+ , \chi^{(2) _{0}$ being $\pm \pi/2$, which is expected as $x$ and $y$ axes are orthogonal.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: For linear current, enforcing mirror symmetry will make Hall component vanishing.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the nonlinear case, there are two independent parameters that correspond to Hall response.
elements.sent-2411-265: Having a mirror axis reduces the number of independent parameters from 2 to 1, hence, nonlinear Hall effect is not prohibited by mirror symmetry.
elements.section-2411-003: Nonlinear transport in disk geometry as an electrostatics problem
elements.sent-2411-266: In this section we set up the electrostatics problem which describes the current flow inside the sample in disk geometry.
elements.sent-2411-267: We consider a sample made of conducting material in the shape of a disk with source and drain attached to two arbitrary points on the boundary of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-268: We further assume that both linear and nonlinear conductivity are uniform and that the sample is in diffusive regime.
elements.sent-2411-269: These are the only two fundamental assumptions of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-270: We consider the case of source and drain being point-like.
elements.sent-2411-271: Generalization to contacts being of finite size is done by integrating over a distribution of point-like sources.
elements.sent-2411-272: We start by considering the nonlinear Ohm's law Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_complex \begin{equation}j_x + i j_y = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}}(E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right),\end{equation} and the continuity equation for electrical current: \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot j = I \left( \delta (\br - \br_S) - \delta (\br - \br_D) \right).\, \label{Continuity_equation}\end{equation} where $\br_{S,D}$ -- are positions of point-like source and drain correspondingly, and $I$ -- is the integrated current density injected into the sample.}
elements.formula-2411-054: \begin{equation}j_x + i j_y = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x + i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x - i E_y \right) + \sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}}(E_x-iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} (E_x+iE_y)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left( E_x^2+E_y^2 \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-273: We are looking for a potential solution such that the electric field is given by a gradient of the electrostatic potential \begin{equation}E (\br)=-\nabla_{\br} \Phi(\br).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-274: For arbitrary magnitude of the injected current this nonlinear problem is intractable due to severe nonlinearities appearing in the nonlinear Ohm's law equation.
elements.sent-2411-275: For small magnitudes of injected current, however, this problem becomes treatable perturbatively.
elements.sent-2411-276: Consider the power-law expansion of current density in powers of the injected current magnitude $I$: \begin{equation}j_{\alpha} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} j^{(n-1)}_{\alpha},\end{equation} where $j^{(n-1) _{\alpha \propto I^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: We can then look for solutions to the nonlinear problem order by order in $I$.
elements.sent-2411-278: At the first order in $I$ the Ohm's law becomes linear \begin{equation}j_x^{(0)} + i j_y^{(0)} = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x^{(0)} + i E_y^{(0)} \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x^{(0)} - i E_y^{(0)} \right)\end{equation} and the continuity equation contains only $j^{(0)}$: \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot j^{(0)} = I \left( \delta (\br - \br_S) - \delta (\br - \br_D) \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-058: \begin{equation}j_x^{(0)} + i j_y^{(0)} = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( E_x^{(0)} + i E_y^{(0)} \right) + \sigma^{(1)}_{-} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{-}} \left( E_x^{(0)} - i E_y^{(0)} \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-279:
We make use of the linear electrostatic potential definition  egin{equation}E^{(0)} ( r)=-
abla_{ r} \Phi^{(0)}( r),\end{equation} and substitute it into the linear Ohm's law to express the 0th-order current density via the gradient of the 0th-order electrostatic field  egin{equation}j_x^{(0)} + i j_y^{(0)} = -\sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left( 
abla_{x} \Phi^{(0)}( r) + i

elements.formula-2411-061:

elements.sent-2411-280: We now apply divergence operator to both sides of the expression above to combine its right-hand-side with the right-hand-side of the continuity equation.
elements.sent-2411-281:
The result of such operation is a second-order partial differential equation  egin{equation}-\sigma^{(1)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(1)}_{+}} \left(

abla^2_{x} \Phi^{(0)}( r) - \left(  ar{\sigma} - \Delta \sigma ight) 
abla^2_{y} \Phi^{(0)}( r) = I \left( \delta ( r -  r_S) - \delta ( r -  r_D) ight),\end{equation} where  egin{equation} ar{\sigma} + \Delta \sigma = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} + \sigma^{(1)}_{-}, \;\;\;  ar{\sigma} - \Delta \sigma = \sigma^{(1)}_{+} - \sigma^{(1)}_{-}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-282: The boundary condition accompanying this differential equation is based on the constraint that current cannot leave the sample anywhere but at the drain.
elements.sent-2411-283: This requirement forces the boundary condition to be Neumann.
elements.sent-2411-284: We present the explicit form of boundary conditions in the following subsections.
elements.sent-2411-285: We now are seeking $j^{(1)}$, the first correction to the current density due to nonlinear dependence on electric field.
elements.sent-2411-286: This correction stems from having nonlinearity in \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate (or in \eqref{Ohm_in_complex ) due to the nonvanishing nonlinear conductivity tensor.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: To find $j^{(1)}$ we need to consider terms of the order $I^2$ in Ohm's law \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate .}
elements.sent-2411-288: At this order Eq. \eqref{Ohm_in_coordinate reads \begin{equation}j^{(1)}_{\alpha} = \sigma_{\alpha \mu} E_{\mu}^{(1)} + \tilde{\sigma}_{\alpha \mu \nu} E_{\mu}^{(0)} E_{\nu}^{(0)},\end{equation} where $E_{\mu ^{(0)}$ -- is the electric field generated by the electrostatic potential in the linear case (0th-order term, linear in $I$) and $E_{\mu ^{(1)}$ -- is the electric field generated by the correction of the order $I^2$ to the electrostatic potential due to nonlinearity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: The correction to current density $j^{(1)}$ satisfies a trivial continuity equation \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot j^{(1)} = 0\end{equation} as there are no sources at the boundary which are quadratic in $I$.
elements.sent-2411-290: From the physical perspective this corresponds to the fact that nonlinear current is generated within the system itself due to the nonlinearity and not by external sources.
elements.sent-2411-291: In other words, in the perturbative regime the source for corrections to the linear regime at order $n$ stems from the solution at order $n-1$.
elements.sent-2411-292: Because the divergence of $j^{(1)}$ vanishes and because inside the disk the curl of $E^{(1)}$ must vanish (due to path independence of the work done by a unit charge between any two points inside), we can express \begin{equation}E^{(1)} = - \nabla_{\br} \Phi^{(1)} (\br).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-293:
Combining the last two equations together with the definition of nonlinear correction to electrostatic potential $E^{(1)}$ we arrive at the second-order partial differential equation for $\Phi^{(1)}$:  egin{eqnarray} j^{(1)}=\sigma\cdot E^{(1)}+\mathcal{E}^{(2)}=-\sigma\cdot 
abla\Phi^{(1)}+\mathcal{E}^{(2)},

u} E_{\mu}^{(0)} ( r) E_{
u}^{(0)} ( r)\end{equation} -- is the source field for nonlinear potential stemming from the electric fields generated in the linear regime.
elements.sent-2411-294: Note that for $\Delta \sigma=0$ Eq. \eqref{eq:Lap2 becomes the Poisson equation.}
elements.sent-2411-295: For $\Delta \sigma\neq0$ one can perform a transormation of variables to cast \eqref{eq:Lap2 into the Poisson form.}
elements.sent-2411-296: The boundary condition is again of Neumann type.
elements.sent-2411-297: Below we separately discuss the problem setup for the case of isotropic linear conductivity and anisotropic linear conductivity.

elements.subsection-2411-010: The case of isotropic linear conductivity tensor
elements.sent-2411-299: In the case of isotropic linear conductivity tensor \begin{equation}\sigma^{(1)}_{+} = \sigma, \; \; \; \sigma^{(1)}_{-} = \chi^{(1)}_{+} = \chi^{(1)}_{-} = 0,\end{equation} For notational simplicity, we define $\mathcal{E ^{(2)}$ by writing \begin{eqnarray} j^{(1)}=\sigma E^{(1)}+\mathcal{E}^{(2)} \text{where}\;\;\; \mathcal{E}_x^{(2)}+i\mathcal{E}_y^{(2)}=\sigma^{(2)}_{-} e^{i\chi^{(2)}_{-}}\left( E_x^{(0)}-iE_y^{(0)} \right)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{+} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{+}} \left( E_x^{(0)}+iE_y^{(0)} \right)^2 + \sigma^{(2)}_{0} e^{i \chi^{(2)}_{0}} \left[ \left(E_x^{(0)}\right)^2 + \left(E_y^{(0)}\right)^2 \right].\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.subsection-2411-011: The case of anisotropic linear conductivity tensor
